text: The famine was still severe in the land.
When they had eaten all the grain from Egypt, Jacob told his sons to go back and buy a little more food.
Judah said, the man warned us plainly not to see his face unless our brother is with us.
If you'll send Benjamin with us, we will go down and buy food.
But if you won't send him, we won't go, since the man said not to see his face without our brother.
Israel asked why they told the man they had another brother, bringing this trouble on him.

They said the man questioned them closely about themselves and their family, asking if their father was still alive and if they had another brother; they answered his questions, having no way of knowing he'd say to bring the brother down.
Judah said to Israel, send the boy with me, and we will go so we may live and not die, we and you and our children.
I myself will guarantee his safety; hold me responsible; if I don't bring him back, I will bear the blame forever.
If we hadn't delayed, we could have been there and back twice by now.
Israel said, if it must be, take some of the best products of the land as a gift: balm, honey, spices, myrrh, pistachio nuts, and almonds.
Take double the silver, returning the silver put back in your sacks; perhaps it was a mistake.
Take your brother too and go back to the man.
May God Almighty grant you mercy before the man so he sends back Simeon and Benjamin; as for me, if I am bereaved, I am bereaved.
So the men took the gifts, double silver, and Benjamin, and went down to Egypt and stood before Joseph.
When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he told his steward to take the men to his house and prepare a meal, for they would eat with him at noon.
The steward did as Joseph said and took the men to Joseph's house.
They were frightened to be taken there, thinking it was because of the silver returned in their sacks, meaning to attack and enslave them and seize their donkeys.
They approached the steward at the entrance and spoke to him.
Please, sir, they said, we came down the first time only to buy food.
But at the lodging place we opened our sacks and each man's silver was there in full; we've brought it back with us.
We've also brought additional silver to buy food; we don't know who put our silver in our sacks.
It's all right, the steward said, don't be afraid; your God, the God of your father, must have put treasure in your sacks; I received your silver. He then brought Simeon out to them.
The steward took the men into Joseph's house, gave them water to wash their feet, and fed their donkeys.
They prepared their gifts for Joseph's arrival at noon, having heard they would eat there.
When Joseph came home, they presented him with the gifts and bowed to the ground before him.
He asked how they were and whether their aged father they'd spoken of was still living.
They said, your servant our father is still alive and well, and they bowed low again.
Joseph looked and saw his brother Benjamin, his own mother's son, and asked if this was the younger brother they'd told him about, saying God be gracious to you, my son.
Deeply moved at the sight of his brother, Joseph hurried out and looked for a place to weep; he entered his private room and wept there.
He washed his face, came out, and controlling himself, said, serve the food.
They served him by himself, the brothers by themselves, and the Egyptians eating with him separately, since Egyptians could not eat with Hebrews, an abomination to them.
The brothers were seated before him in order by age, from the firstborn to the youngest, and they looked at each other in astonishment.
Portions were served to them from Joseph's table, but Benjamin's portion was five times as much as anyone else's; they drank freely and celebrated with him.
